1: Return, O Israel, to the LORD your God — shuva Yisra'el 'ad YHWH elohekha: the imperative shuv (return, turn back) is the covenant-renewal call. The preposition is 'ad (toward, as far as) rather than the more common el — a nuance suggesting completeness of return, all the way back. ki kashalta ba'avonekha — because you have stumbled in your iniquity. The reason given for the call is the very stumbling itself: the fall is grounds for the invitation, not disqualification from it. This pivot from Hosea 13:16's carnage to the restoration appeal is without transition — the abruptness is the point.
2: Take words with you and return — qeḥu immakhem devarim ve-shuvu 'el YHWH. Qeḥu is the Qal imperative plural of laqaḥ (take). The instruction to bring words (verbal offerings, not animal sacrifices) is distinctive: this is a prayer-liturgy, not a ritual. The words prescribed follow: 'Take away all iniquity; accept what is good; and we will offer as payment the bulls of our lips.' Parim sefatenu — the bulls of our lips — transforms sacrifice into speech-act; lips replace the slaughtered animal. This anticipates Hebrews 13:15 ('a sacrifice of praise — the fruit of lips'). Devarim as the vehicle of return also anticipates Jeremiah 31:33 (the word written on the heart).
3: Assyria will not save us — Ashshur lo' yoshi'enu: the same moshia' root from Hosea 13:4, now applied to Assyria — with explicit denial. The three renunciations form a complete rejection of the false triad: (1) foreign military power (Assyria), (2) military hardware ('al-susim lo' nirkav — we will not ride on horses), and (3) idol-making (ve-lo'-nomar 'od elohenu le-ma'aseh yadenu — we will not again say 'our god' to the work of our hands). The renunciation of horse-warfare echoes the royal law of Deuteronomy 17:16. The idol-renunciation directly undoes the calf-kiss of Hosea 13:2. Ki bekha yeruḥam yatom — for in you the orphan finds mercy: the closing ground shifts from human resolution to divine compassion.
4: I will heal their waywardness — erpa' meshuvotam: YHWH's response begins. Meshuvah (waywardness, apostasy, turning away) is the noun form of shuv — turning from YHWH. The divine commitment is to heal the very act of turning away. Ahavem nedabah — I will love them freely/voluntarily. Nedabah is the freewill offering (nedabah) of Leviticus — given without compulsion, beyond what is required, from pure generosity. Applied to divine love, it means YHWH's affection is not earned or coerced but freely bestowed. Ki shav appi mimmennu — for my anger has turned from them. The reversal of the anger idiom is precise: the same shuv Israel is called to perform (v1-2) is the word for YHWH's anger turning away.
5: I will be like dew to Israel — ehyeh ke-tal le-Yisra'el: the botanical restoration begins. Tal (dew) is the critical contrast term: in Hosea 13:3, Israel's love (ḥesed) was 'like the early dew that goes away' — impermanent. Now YHWH's own presence is the dew — but it sustains rather than vanishes. Yifrach kashoshannah — he will blossom like a lily. The lily (shoshannah) is the flower of spring after desert heat; it also appears in Song of Songs Hosea 2:1-2 for the beloved. Ve-yakh shereshav ka-Levanon — he will strike root like Lebanon. Lebanon's cedars were proverbially deep-rooted; rootedness is the stability denied in the exile-imagery of previous chapters.
6: His branches will spread — yelekhु yonekotav: the shoot-imagery (young branches, suckers) of an olive or vine. Ve-yhi ke-zayit hodo — his splendor will be like the olive tree. The olive was Israel's prestige crop: long-lived, perennial, the source of oil for food, light, and anointing. Ve-reyḥo lo ka-Levanon — his fragrance like Lebanon. Lebanon's famous cedar fragrance (resinous, durable) is now applied to restored Israel — the tree that was stripped and scattered will bear the scent of permanence. The fragrance of Lebanon appears also in Song of Songs Hosea 4:11.
7: Those who dwell in his shade will return — yashovu yoshevei be-tsillo: shuv appears again — the root-word of the entire chapter. Those who shelter under the restored Israel-tree will themselves return. Yeḥayu dagan — they will grow grain. Ve-yifsetu ka-gafen zikro — his memory/fame will blossom like a vine. Zikro (his remembrance) — the renown of restored Israel will spread like a vine in full bearing. The agricultural metaphors accumulate: lily → cedar → olive → grain → vine, a complete harvest of the land's produce, reversing the agricultural curses of the earlier chapters.
8: Ephraim, what more do I have to do with idols — Ephrayim mah-li 'od la-'atsabbim: YHWH addresses Ephraim directly. The question is rhetorical — Ephraim now has nothing to do with idols. The verse then becomes textually complex: ani 'aniti va'ashurenu — most read 'I am the one who answers him and I watch over him.' Aniti from 'anah (answer/respond); ashurenu from shur (look, observe). The botanical climax follows: ani ke-verosh ra'anan — I am like a flourishing cypress tree. YHWH compared to a specific tree (verosh, cypress or juniper) is unique in the Hebrew Bible; it may play against the idolatry of cutting trees for idols (Isaiah 44:14 uses tirzah and erez). Mimmenni peryekha nimtsa' — from me your fruit is found. The source of Israel's fruitfulness is YHWH himself, not the land, the Baals, or the idols.
9: Who is wise, let him understand these things — mi ḥakham ve-yaven 'elleh: the book closes with a wisdom call, moving from prophetic oracle to sapiential reflection. Navonim ve-yeda'um — the discerning will know them. Ki yesharim darkei YHWH — for the ways of YHWH are right (yashar, straight, upright). The final line encapsulates the book's moral structure: u-tsaddiqim yelkhu vam (the righteous walk in them) vs. u-pose'im yikkashlu vam (the rebellious stumble in them). The same paths lead to life for one and ruin for the other. Yikkashlu (stumble/fall) echoes kashalta (you have stumbled) of Hosea 14:1, closing the book with a structural inclusio: the call to return from stumbling in v1 is answered by the observation that those who refuse the ways of YHWH will continue to stumble.
